caption:
Human: Microscopic description: The lesion is a well-differentiated neuroendocrine neoplasm, characterized by a complex architecture of irregular nests and sheets. Cells exhibit a spindle shape with moderate pleomorphism, and have prominent nucleoli. Mitotic figures are scattered throughout the lesion.
Key supporting features: Irregular nests and sheets, spindle-shaped cells, moderate pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, scattered mitotic figures.
Likely interpretation: The lesion is consistent with a neuroendocrine neoplasm, possibly a neuroendocrine carcinoma or a variant thereof, given the complex architecture and cytological features.

caption:
Microscopic description: The lesion is a nodular growth with irregular borders, infiltrating the surrounding tissue. It consists of spindle-shaped cells with moderate pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and occasional mitotic figures. The cytoplasm is variably pigmented.
Key supporting features: Nodular growth pattern; irregular borders; infiltration; spindle-shaped cells; moderate pleomorphism; prominent nucleoli; mitotic figures; cytoplasmic pigmentation.
Likely interpretation: The lesion is consistent with a nodular variant of melanocytic neoplasms, such as a melanoma or a dysplastic nevus

image: /raid/jupyter-annabeth.lu.md12/Anna_CONCH/ver-26-arvo/data/hail_mary_0501/S109-03375_Uveal melanoma/spindle A cell type has nuclear grooving (arrow).jpg
caption:
Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection is a common cause of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN). CIN is characterized by the presence of dysplastic cells within the epithelium. Dysplastic cells are typically larger, more pleomorphic, and have increased nuclear size compared to normal cells. They may also exhibit nuclear grooves, mitotic figures, and increased cytoplasmic pigmentation. The architecture of CIN can vary from nests of cells to sheets or fascicles, depending on the stage of the disease.
